questions.question: Which Latin phrase expresses the maxim 'equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy'?

options.A: Ubi jus ibi remedium
options.B: Aequitas sequitur legem
options.C: Ex turpi causa non oritur actio
options.D: Nemo dat quod non habet

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy' is expressed in the Latin phrase 'ubi jus ibi remedium', meaning 'where there is a right, there is a remedy'. This is the foundational maxim explaining why equity developed — to fill gaps left by the rigidity of the common law.

questions.question: The maxim 'equity follows the law' was established in which case?

options.A: Walsh v Lonsdale [1882]
options.B: Earl of Oxford's Case [1615]
options.C: Moody v Cox [1917]
options.D: Feeney v MacManus [1937]

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity follows the law' derives from the Earl of Oxford's Case [1615]. It establishes that equity does not exist to overthrow or replace the common law but to supplement it where there is a gap or deficiency. Where the common law provides an adequate remedy, equity will not interfere.

questions.question: The doctrine of laches reflects which equitable maxim?

options.A: He who seeks equity must do equity
options.B: Equality is equity
options.C: Delay defeats equity
options.D: Equity acts in personam

questions.explanation: The doctrine of laches reflects the maxim 'delay defeats equity'. It provides that if a party sits on their rights for too long, the court may refuse equitable relief. Laches is an equitable defence distinct from statutory limitation periods and can bar a claim even within the limitation period where delay has been unreasonable and prejudicial.

questions.question: The clean hands doctrine requires that the applicant's misconduct must be:

options.A: Of a criminal nature before the court will take notice
options.B: Connected to the specific transaction or relief sought
options.C: Disclosed to the respondent before proceedings are issued
options.D: Admitted in the pleadings to be raised as a defence

questions.explanation: Under the clean hands doctrine, the applicant's misconduct must be connected to the specific transaction or relief sought. General bad behaviour is not sufficient to deny equitable relief — the unclean hands must relate to the matter before the court. This was confirmed in Moody v Cox and applied in the Irish case of Murphy Buckley and Keogh v Pye Ireland.

questions.question: The rule in Walsh v Lonsdale [1882] illustrates which equitable maxim?

options.A: Equity acts in personam
options.B: Equality is equity
options.C: Equity regards as done that which ought to be done
options.D: He who comes to equity must come with clean hands

questions.explanation: The rule in Walsh v Lonsdale [1882] illustrates the maxim 'equity regards as done that which ought to be done'. In that case, an agreement for a lease was treated as equivalent to an actual lease in equity. The maxim allows equity to look at substance over form — where a party has an obligation to do something, equity will treat it as already done.

questions.question: In Feeney v MacManus [1937], which equitable maxim was applied to divide surplus funds equally among members?

options.A: Equity will not perfect an imperfect gift
options.B: Equality is equity
options.C: Delay defeats equity
options.D: He who seeks equity must do equity

questions.explanation: Feeney v MacManus [1937] illustrates the maxim 'equality is equity'. Where there is no basis for distinguishing between competing claims, equity will divide equally. In this case, surplus funds of a dissolved unincorporated association were divided equally among existing members.

questions.question: The maxim 'equity acts in personam' means that the Court of Chancery historically enforced its orders against:

options.A: The property of the defendant directly through sequestration
options.B: The defendant personally, through their conscience
options.C: Third parties who held assets on behalf of the defendant
options.D: The Crown as ultimate enforcer of equitable obligations

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity acts in personam' means that the Court of Chancery enforced its orders not against property directly but against the person — the defendant's conscience. Disobeying an order of the Chancellor resulted in personal penalties, including imprisonment for contempt. This personal jurisdiction gave equity its unique character.

questions.question: Which maxim underpins the doctrines of satisfaction and ademption?

options.A: Equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy
options.B: Where the equities are equal, the first in time prevails
options.C: Equity imputes an intention to fulfil an obligation
options.D: Equity looks to the intent rather than the form

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity imputes an intention to fulfil an obligation' underpins the doctrines of satisfaction and ademption. It means that where a person is under an obligation and subsequently does an act referable to that obligation, equity will presume they intended to fulfil it.

questions.question: Which statement BEST describes the relationship between equity and the common law?

options.A: Equity displaces the common law wherever there is a conflict
options.B: Equity supplements the common law by filling gaps and remedying deficiencies
options.C: Equity and common law operate as entirely separate systems with no interaction
options.D: Equity only applies in criminal proceedings where statutory penalties are inadequate

questions.explanation: Equity supplements the common law by filling gaps and remedying deficiencies — it does not overthrow or replace it. The maxim 'equity follows the law' establishes this relationship. Equity only acts where the common law provides an inadequate remedy.

questions.question: The maxim 'he who seeks equity must do equity' requires the applicant to:

options.A: Have obtained independent legal advice before applying to the court
options.B: Be prepared to act fairly and deal justly with the opposing party
options.C: Pay costs in advance as a condition of receiving equitable relief
options.D: Waive any right to damages at common law before seeking an injunction

questions.explanation: 'He who seeks equity must do equity' requires that if a party is asking the court for an equitable remedy, they must be prepared to act fairly and deal justly with the other party. It is a condition of equitable relief — the applicant cannot seek the court's assistance while behaving unconscionably towards the respondent.

questions.question: Which priority rule applies where both parties have competing equitable interests and neither has behaved inequitably?

options.A: Where the equities are equal, the law prevails
options.B: Where the equities are equal, the first in time prevails
options.C: Equality is equity and the interests are divided equally
options.D: The court will refuse to adjudicate and dismiss the proceedings

questions.explanation: Where two parties have competing equitable interests and neither has behaved better or worse than the other, the maxim 'where the equities are equal, the first in time prevails' applies. The interest that arose first in time will succeed. If one party has a legal interest and the other an equitable interest only, then 'where the equities are equal, the law prevails' applies.

questions.question: In Murphy Buckley and Keogh v Pye Ireland, the clean hands doctrine was applied to:

options.A: Grant the applicant specific performance despite minor procedural irregularities
options.B: Refuse equitable relief where the applicant's conduct was tainted
options.C: Award damages in lieu of an injunction to the applicant
options.D: Compel the respondent to account for secret profits

questions.explanation: In the Irish case of Murphy Buckley and Keogh v Pye Ireland, the clean hands doctrine was applied to refuse equitable relief where the applicant's conduct was tainted. This is a leading Irish application of the doctrine, confirming that a party seeking equitable relief must not be guilty of unconscionable conduct connected to the matter in dispute.

questions.question: Which of the following CORRECTLY distinguishes laches from a statutory limitation period?

options.A: Laches applies only in commercial disputes whereas limitation periods apply to all claims
options.B: Laches is an equitable defence and can bar a claim even within the statutory limitation period if delay was unreasonable and caused prejudice
options.C: A statutory limitation period is discretionary whereas laches is an absolute bar
options.D: Laches applies only to claims in personam and limitation periods apply only to claims in rem

questions.explanation: Laches is a separate equitable defence that can bar a claim even within the statutory limitation period if the delay has been unreasonable and has caused prejudice to the respondent. Unlike a statutory limitation period, laches is discretionary — the court assesses whether the claimant slept on their rights in a manner that makes it inequitable to grant relief.

questions.question: The maxim 'equity looks to the intent rather than the form' means that equity will:

options.A: Accept oral evidence in all cases regardless of statutory formality requirements
options.B: Look behind the legal form of a transaction to its true substance and the actual intention of the parties
options.C: Require strict compliance with written formalities before granting any equitable remedy
options.D: Disregard written agreements in favour of conduct as a means of proving intention

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity looks to the intent rather than the form' means equity will look behind the legal form of a transaction to its true substance. For example, a conveyance dressed up to disguise its nature as a security will be treated by equity as a mortgage, regardless of its formal documentation.

questions.question: For an FE-1 question involving a party who has delayed asserting their rights, which TWO maxims are most immediately relevant?

options.A: Equality is equity and equity acts in personam
options.B: Clean hands and delay defeats equity (laches)
options.C: Equity follows the law and equity will not perfect an imperfect gift
options.D: Equity imputes an intention to fulfil an obligation and where equities are equal the first in time prevails

questions.explanation: Where a party has delayed asserting their rights, the most immediately relevant maxims are 'delay defeats equity' (laches) and the clean hands doctrine. Laches directly addresses unreasonable delay. The clean hands doctrine is also potentially relevant where the delay itself reflects inequitable conduct on the applicant's part.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the traditional equitable maxims forming the philosophical DNA of equity?

options.A: Equity looks to the intent rather than the form
options.B: Equity will not assist a volunteer
options.C: Where the equities are equal, the law prevails
options.D: Equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy

questions.explanation: 'Equity will not assist a volunteer' is a principle from the law of trusts — specifically, the constitution of trusts (Milroy v Lord [1862]) — rather than a foundational equitable maxim in the classical sense. While closely associated with equity, it is a trust law rule rather than one of the general equitable maxims.

questions.question: An applicant for equitable relief has engaged in fraud in an entirely unrelated commercial transaction. Which statement BEST reflects the application of the clean hands doctrine?

options.A: The applicant will be denied all equitable relief because their general character is dishonest
options.B: The doctrine will only bar relief if the unrelated fraud is connected to the specific matter before the court
options.C: The court must refer the matter to the DPP before granting any relief
options.D: The applicant is automatically barred because fraud is always sufficient to invoke clean hands

questions.explanation: The clean hands doctrine only bars equitable relief where the applicant's misconduct is connected to the specific transaction or relief sought. An unrelated fraud, however serious, will not ordinarily bar equitable relief in a different matter. The misconduct must directly relate to the matter in hand — general bad character is insufficient.

questions.question: The equitable maxims are best described in their role in FE-1 exam answers as:

options.A: Standalone answers to be listed in full in every equity question
options.B: Guiding principles to be woven naturally into the analysis across multiple topics
options.C: Rules with fixed outcomes that determine all equity cases automatically
options.D: Statutory provisions contained in the Judicature Acts that must be cited verbatim

questions.explanation: The equitable maxims are guiding principles to be woven naturally into analysis across multiple topics — injunctions, trusts, estoppel, specific performance, and more. They are not typically examined as standalone answers. The candidate must identify which maxims are relevant to the facts and apply them within the analysis, demonstrating how they operate in practice.

questions.question: Where the equities are equal and one party has a legal interest while the other has only an equitable interest, which maxim applies?

options.A: Where the equities are equal, the first in time prevails
options.B: Where the equities are equal, the law prevails
options.C: Equality is equity and interests are divided equally
options.D: Equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy

questions.explanation: Where the equities are equal and one party has a legal interest while the other has only an equitable interest, the maxim 'where the equities are equal, the law prevails' applies — the legal interest takes priority. Only where both interests are equitable and neither party has behaved inequitably does the first-in-time rule apply.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios MOST clearly invokes the maxim 'equity regards as done that which ought to be done'?

options.A: A trustee who distributes trust funds to incorrect beneficiaries
options.B: An agreement for a lease being treated as an actual lease in equity
options.C: A donor delaying completion of a gift until after their death
options.D: A creditor waiting several years before enforcing a debt

questions.explanation: 'Equity regards as done that which ought to be done' is most clearly illustrated by the rule in Walsh v Lonsdale [1882], where an agreement for a lease was treated as equivalent to an actual lease in equity. Where a party has an obligation to do something, equity will treat it as already done.

questions.question: Equity developed historically because the common law was characterised as:

options.A: Too discretionary and unpredictable, undermining commercial certainty
options.B: Too recent a development to have adequate remedies for all situations
options.C: Rigid, formulaic and often leaving deserving parties without a remedy
options.D: Excessively focused on equitable interests at the expense of legal titles

questions.explanation: Equity developed because the common law was rigid, formulaic, and often left deserving parties without recourse. The Court of Chancery developed equitable principles to address the shortcomings of the common law — particularly where strict legal rules produced unjust outcomes. This is the historical justification for the maxim 'equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy'.

questions.question: An applicant seeks a mandatory injunction but has failed to pursue their claim promptly after discovering the breach. Which maxim provides the respondent with the strongest defence?

options.A: Equity acts in personam
options.B: Equity looks to the intent rather than the form
options.C: Delay defeats equity (laches)
options.D: Where the equities are equal, the law prevails

questions.explanation: The maxim 'delay defeats equity' (laches) provides the strongest defence where an applicant has failed to pursue their claim promptly. Even a valid equitable claim can be barred if the claimant unreasonably delayed in asserting their rights and that delay caused prejudice to the respondent. In injunction proceedings, laches is a frequently raised equitable defence.
